serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw yellow tomato weighs approximately 123 g and is typically consumed whole or sliced as a snack or salad ingredient.

nutrient_source.title: Tomatoes, yellow, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Values preserved as supplied. Cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697 (Tomatoes, yellow, raw) which reports energy 15 kcal, protein 1.0 g, fat 0.3 g, carbohydrate 2.8 g per 100 g — consistent with HPB values within rounding tolerance. Secondary micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Yellow tomato GI is not separately listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated from the published GI of red tomato (GI ~15, low GI category) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Yellow tomatoes share essentially the same carbohydrate profile and sugar composition as red tomatoes. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 2.98 × 1.23) / 100 ≈ 0.55, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100 g) and high potassium (258 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw yellow tomato is highly suitable for hypertension management; its very low sodium content and high potassium (258 mg/100 g) support the DASH dietary pattern and contribute to blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.98 g/100 g), low GI (~15), and low glycemic load per serving (~0.6)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw yellow tomato is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its very low carbohydrate content, low GI of approximately 15, and negligible glycemic load per serving pose minimal risk to postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and beneficial potassium and antioxidant content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw yellow tomato is cardioprotective; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant compounds (vitamin C, vitamin E, carotenoids) support cardiovascular health and are consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade classification — solanine alkaloids may exacerbate joint inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yellow tomato is a nightshade vegetable; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint symptom flares after consumption, as solanine alkaloids present in nightshades are a recognised concern for RA-susceptible individuals, though evidence remains mixed and tolerance is individual.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw yellow tomato (Solanum lycopersicum, yellow variety) is a whole, unprocessed fruit botanically classified as a berry but universally consumed as a vegetable in culinary practice. It is distinguished from the common red tomato by its bright golden-yellow skin and flesh, which result from a higher concentration of carotenoids such as lutein and beta-carotene rather than lycopene. The flavour profile is notably milder and sweeter than red tomatoes, with reduced acidity and a soft, juicy texture that makes it particularly appealing eaten raw. No cooking or processing is applied; the fruit is simply washed and consumed whole, halved, or sliced. In global culinary contexts, raw yellow tomato appears in fresh salads, antipasto platters, grain bowls, and as a colourful garnish or snack. It is widely used in Mediterranean, European, and contemporary fusion cuisines and is available year-round in most markets. From a nutritional standpoint, yellow tomato is exceptionally low in calories (15 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (2.98 g per 100 g), with negligible fat (0.26 g per 100 g) and modest protein (0.98 g per 100 g). It delivers meaningful potassium (258 mg per 100 g), vitamin C (23.4 mg per 100 g), vitamin E (0.54 mg per 100 g), folate (15 mcg DFE per 100 g), and a range of B vitamins. Its very low sodium (23 mg per 100 g) and high potassium content make it strongly aligned with the DASH dietary pattern recommended for hypertension management. The low glycemic index (approximately 15) and negligible glycemic load per serving make it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-GI dietary protocols. The negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health. As a nightshade vegetable, yellow tomato contains solanine alkaloids that may be a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to this compound class, though individual tolerance varies considerably. Yellow tomato is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher, and is suitable for low-sodium, low-calorie, and borderline ketogenic dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is yellow tomato good for diabetes?
How many calories in a yellow tomato?
Can I eat yellow tomato if I have high blood pressure?
Yellow tomato vs red tomato nutrition
What vitamins are in yellow tomato?
Is yellow tomato good for heart health?

Core macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697 (Tomatoes, yellow, raw) within rounding tolerance. Secondary nutrient values (saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, sugar, vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697 as a cross-reference supplement. GI estimated from published red tomato GI (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) as no separate yellow tomato GI entry exists.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as supplied in the input record (confidence 0.95, reviewed). 2. Secondary nutrient values (saturated_fat_g 0.04, monounsaturated_fat_g 0.04, polyunsaturated_fat_g 0.11, trans_fat_g 0.0, sugar_g 2.49, vitamin_c_mg 23.4, vitamin_a_mcg_rae 17.0, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg 0.04, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg 0.02, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg 0.59, vitamin_b6_mg 0.08, vitamin_b12_mcg 0.0, folate_mcg_dfe 15.0, vitamin_d_mcg 0.0, vitamin_e_mg 0.54, phosphorus_mg 28.0, magnesium_mg 9.0, zinc_mg 0.13, selenium_mcg 0.4, water_g 94.5) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697 (Tomatoes, yellow, raw) as a supplementary approved source. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: yellow tomato is a plant food and inherently contains no vitamin B12, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: yellow tomato contains negligible vitamin D, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central NDB 11697. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero for a raw plant food. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on published red tomato GI from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no separate yellow tomato GI entry exists in the University of Sydney database. 5. Ontology: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Tomatoes) is an exact match at genus level; FoodOn FOODON_03301710 is a closest_parent match (tomato whole, yellow variety not separately coded); SNOMED CT 734881000 is a closest_parent match. 6. No mandatory multilingual names required as this is a generic global food item, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. 7. Keto dietary tag from input preserved — at 2.98 g net carbohydrate per 100 g, yellow tomato is borderline keto-compatible in moderate portions. 8. Rheumatoid arthritis health context preserved from input as an additional condition.
